coredata.dc:title: Formaldehyde detection based on tannin carbon dots

Chi, Chuanxi
Zhao, Yue
Jiang, Guiquan
Wu, Junhua
Song, Jianxi
coredata.dc:description: With the acceleration of industrialization, formaldehyde detection is becoming increasingly important. Numerous challenges persist about sensitivity and safety in the field of formaldehyde detection. This paper developed a formaldehyde detection material based on carbon dots, and effectively improved the sensitivity, safety and convenience of detection. Specifically, tannin acid (TA) was utilized as the carbon source and urea as the nitrogen source to prepare tannin/urea carbon dots (TA-CDs) with excellent performance. The fluorescence intensity of the carbon dot solution gradually decreased with increasing formaldehyde concentration. A significant linear relationship (R2 = 0.99462) was observed in the range of 0.00015–0.035 mmol/L, with a practical formaldehyde detection limit of 0.296 μmol/L. This paper utilizes natural poly-phenol compound TA as the raw material, which boasts unique characteristics and low cost. TA-CDs prepared from it are abundant in surface defects and active sites, exhibiting significant advantages in formaldehyde detection and possessing immense application potential.

Carbon Dots
Formaldehyde
Detection

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Different reaction temperatures(a), times(b), and ratios(c) affect fluorescence.
TEM image, scale bar is 20 nm for (a), scale bar is 50 nm for (b), lattice distance for (c), (d) showing particle size distribution.
TEM image, the ratio of TA to urea is 1:0 (a), 1:2 (b), 1:4 (c), 1:8 (d), when scale bar is 20 nm.
FT-IR spectra of TA and TA-CDs.
XRD spectrum of the sample, TA-CDs (a), JCPDS card numbers: 98–000-0231 (b).
UV Absorption of TA(a) and TA-CDs(b).

Optimal excitation and emission peaks(a), inset shows TA-CDs under sunlight and 365 nm UV light illumination. (b) shows fluorescence intensity of TA-CDs under various excitations, and (c) illustrates fluorescence variation of TA-CDs in solutions with different pH values. (d) depicts fluorescence intensity of TA-CDs at different concentrations,

The fluorescence emission spectrum of the TA-CDs/formaldehyde mixed solution under 365 nm excitation (a) and inset shows the fluorescence comparison of TA-CDs aqueous solution under 365 nm UV irradiation before and after formaldehyde addition, (b)a direct relationship between F0/F and formaldehyde concentration, (c) the fitted curve of F0/F versus formaldehyde concentration in the range of 0.00015–0.035 mmol/L demonstrates this relationship.
Comparison of the performance of different carbon points used to detect formaldehyde.

row.entry: Exposure Limit indicated by World Health Organization

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Comparison of performance among different formaldehyde detection methods.

row.entry: Electrochemical Biosensor Based on Enzyme Catalysis

head.ce:title: Formaldehyde detection based on tannin carbon dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Wooden Materials Science and Engineering of Jilin Province, Beihua University, Jilin 132013, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Materials Science and Engineering of Jilin Province

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Wooden Materials Science and Engineering of Jilin Province, Beihua University, Jilin 132013, China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory for the Structure and Function of Polysaccharides in Traditional Chinese Medicine (Administration of Traditional Chinese Medicine of Jilin Province), Beihua University, Jilin 132013, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory for the Structure and Function of Polysaccharides in Traditional Chinese Medicine (Administration of Traditional Chinese Medicine of Jilin Province)

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory for the Structure and Function of Polysaccharides in Traditional Chinese Medicine (Administration of Traditional Chinese Medicine of Jilin Province), Beihua University, Jilin 132013, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Preparation of TA-CDs and its detection of formaldehyde.

With the acceleration of industrialization, formaldehyde detection is becoming increasingly important. Numerous challenges persist about sensitivity and safety in the field of formaldehyde detection. This paper developed a formaldehyde detection material based on carbon dots, and effectively improved the sensitivity, safety and convenience of detection. Specifically, tannin acid (TA) was utilized as the carbon source and urea as the nitrogen source to prepare tannin/urea carbon dots (TA-CDs) with excellent performance. The fluorescence intensity of the carbon dot solution gradually decreased with increasing formaldehyde concentration. A significant linear relationship (R2 = 0.99462) was observed in the range of 0.00015–0.035 mmol/L, with a practical formaldehyde detection limit of 0.296 μmol/L. This paper utilizes natural poly-phenol compound TA as the raw material, which boasts unique characteristics and low cost. TA-CDs prepared from it are abundant in surface defects and active sites, exhibiting significant advantages in formaldehyde detection and possessing immense application potential.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The datasets generated or analyzed during this study are available from the corresponding author on reasonable request.

ce:section.ce:para: With the acceleration of urbanization and industrialization, environmental pollution has become increasingly severe. Air pollutants and their detection have emerged as a significant focus of current research. Among these pollutants, formaldehyde is the most common indoor gas, posing a serious threat to both the environment and human health. Its toxicity can lead to diseases and even death (Liteplo and Meek, 2003). Despite the critical need, effective methods for handling and detecting indoor formaldehyde remain elusive. Researchers are striving to develop methods with high selectivity and sensitivity for on-site formaldehyde detection (Renshan et al., 2023). To address these challenges, various techniques are being employed, including high-performance liquid chromatography-mass spectroscopy (Bhowmik et al., 2017), gas chromatography-mass spectroscopy (Bagheri et al., 2009), spectroscopy (Hou et al., 2024), and chemical reagent methods (Min et al., 2023). However, developing appropriate sensing materials for these techniques presents another significant challenge for scientists. Previous studies have demonstrated that certain fluorescent materials are effectively used in biological imaging (Yu et al., 2024), drug delivery (Hu et al., 2024), and photoelectric equipment (Hu et al., 2024) due to their excellent fluorescence properties. Carbon dots are renowned for their excellent fluorescence properties and simple preparation process, and their unique fluorescence characteristics have been widely applied in various fields.

Carbon quantum dots (CDs) are a novel carbon material characterized by a spherical structure with a size smaller than 10 nm and a core–shell architecture (Mengyuan, 2022).The core consists of a hybridized carbon structure of sp2/sp3, surrounded by a chain-like arrangement of small functional groups and polymers. CDs exhibit excellent water solubility, bio-compatibility, and fluorescence properties. They are also notable for their wide availability, simple preparation methods, non-toxicity, and extensive application value (Wei et al., 2021; Shen et al., 2019; Yuan, 2019). Methods for preparing carbon dots are categorized into two main approaches: top-down and bottom-up. The top-down (Nagarajan et al., 2023; Jian et al., 2024; Wang et al., 2023) method involves breaking down larger carbon materials into smaller particles, which is often a complex and inefficient process. In contrast, the bottom-up method synthesizes carbon sources into tiny particles with fluorescent properties, offering a more straightforward preparation process. Hydrothermal synthesis is the most prevalent technique in the bottom-up approach. This method rarely uses organic solvents, and the reaction time and temperature are critical factors that determine the extent of the reaction. Consequently, higher quantum yields of carbon dots are typically achieved through hydrothermal synthesis. CDs-based sensing and detection techniques rely primarily on their fluorescent behavior (Ferjani et al., 2024); making them valuable in various applications.

The photoluminescence(PL) of CDs is primarily determined by their structure and surface state (Niu et al., 2018). The surface state and the size of sp2 domains can be manipulated by altering the reaction solvent during the preparation process. Additionally, graphitization, surface functionalization, or surface oxidation can be controlled by adjusting the mass ratio of precursors in the solvent reaction. Simple heteroatom doping and surface passivation can significantly enhance the quantum yield (QY) of CDs, thereby improving their fluorescence quantum yield. For instance, CDs synthesized by incorporating elements such as phosphorus, nitrogen, and carbon sources exhibit very high fluorescence quantum yields and stability (Ahmed Abdel Hamid et al., 2023; Li et al., 2017; Pan et al., 2017; Li et al., 2023). Furthermore, the PL of CDs is influenced by sub-fluorescent groups such as C = O, C = N, N = O, C-O, and –NH2 (Zhu et al., 2015). The size of the sp2 conjugated structural domains can also be controlled by adjusting the pH of deionized water in the hydrothermal reaction. Although the preparation of CDs is well established and their applications are extensive, there is still a need to expand their use in gas detection. However, aggregation-induced quenching (AIQ) of CDs emission poses a challenge for their practical application in solid-state light-emitting devices (Hu et al., 2024). To address AIQ, significant efforts have been made to develop suitable CDs-based composites. Techniques such as gel column chromatography (Zhao et al., 2022), solid-phase extraction (Li et al., 2024), and thin-layer chromatography (Parambil et al., 2024) have been employed as matrices capable of dispersing and isolating CDs, thereby reducing interference and preventing AIQ.

Currently, organic synthetic compounds and inorganic metal compounds are commonly used as raw materials for the preparation of amino carbon dots. However, with the advancement of research on carbon dots, there is a growing shift towards using natural product extracts as precursors. Examples of such natural products include rutin (Yanan et al., 2023), amino acids (Zhixin et al., 2024), and tea polyphenols (Huiping, 2023). The diversity and variety of natural products enable the preparation of carbon dot materials with different properties and applications, catering to the needs of various fields. Despite these advantages, the good stability and complexity of natural products pose challenges for chemical reaction process analysis.

In this paper, we report the preparation of nitrogen-doped carbon dots with amino functional groups using TA and urea as precursors, and water as a solvent. TA, a natural product, is structurally rich and contains numerous conjugated structures that facilitate the transfer of π electrons throughout the molecule. TA-CDs prepared from it are abundant in surface defects and active sites, exhibiting significant advantages in formaldehyde detection and possessing immense application potential.
Dichloromethane (DCM 99.9 %), methanol (MeOH 99 %), tannic acid (TA 90 %), urea (99 %), and formaldehyde (37 %) were obtained from Energy Chemical. Deionized water was used throughout the experiment.

Take 0.25 g of TA and 1.6 g of urea as the precursor, add 10 mL of deionized water, stir well to dissolve all the tannic acid and urea, put the solution into a reactor, and heat it at 180 °C for 6 h, to obtain a black TA-CDs solution with a small amount of black precipitate. The black precipitate was removed by centrifugation at 5000 r for 10 min to obtain a brown-black tannin/urea carbon point solution.

The black TA-CDs solution prepared in the previous step was separated and purified by column chromatography using a mixture of dichloromethane: methanol: water = 3: 2: 0.5 as the mobile phase. The purified solution was evaporated to remove impurities using a rotary evaporator, and then freeze-dried to produce the purified TA-CDs powder. The solution was configured to 20 mg/mL and set aside.

For the preparation of CDs at high temperature and high pressure, a high-pressure reactor (Jinan Fair-price Chemical Co., Ltd., China) and a vacuum drying oven (DZF-6090, Shanghai Yiheng Scientific Instruments Co., Ltd., China) were employed using the one-pot method.

The carbon dots were then diluted to a concentration of 2 mg/mL. A small amount of this solution was placed into a capillary tube and subsequently dropped onto a copper grid. Transmission electron microscope (TEM) images from Phillip, Netherlands, were used to observe the size and distribution of the carbon dots.

The lattice structure of the carbon dots was analyzed using a D/max-2500VL/PC X-ray diffractometer (XRD) from Rigaku Co., Ltd, Japan. The carbon dot powder was fully ground and pressed into a film for this analysis. The XRD test was conducted using Cu-targeted Kα radiation, with a scanning speed of 4°/min, a scanning range of 5-40°, a filament current of 30 mA, and an accelerating voltage of 40 kV.
Surface analyses were performed with a THERMO X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS) from Thermo Electron Corporation, USA. High-resolution scans were conducted for the elements C, O, and N.

After purification, 1 mg of carbon dots was added to optical potassium bromide, fully ground with an agate grinder, pressed into tablets, and scanned over a range of 4000–400 cm−1 using a VERTEX 80 V Fourier Transform Infrared Spectrometer (FT-IR) from Bruker, Germany.

The UV absorption peaks of the carbon dots were tested using Varian's Cary 5000 Ultraviolet–Visible-Near Infrared (UV-NIR) Spectrophotometer. The solvent UV absorption peaks were subtracted first, and the test range was 200–800 nm.
The emission intensity of the carbon dots was checked using the Cary Eclipse Fluorescence Spectrophotometer, with excitation peaks ranging from 200-400 nm and emission peaks ranging from 200-600 nm.

Quinine sulfate (0.1 M H2SO4 as solvent; QY = 0.54) was chosen as standard. The quantum yield of CDs in water was calculated. The QY was determined using the slope method (Wang et al., 2020): the integrated photoluminescence intensity (360 nm excitation) and absorbance values of the samples were compared with those of the reference. Then used equation:
(1)
Q x
/
Q
st

S x
S

A
st
A x
(
η x
η
st
)
2

Q is QY, S is the integrated area from the emission spectrum, A is the absorbance value and η is the refractive index. The subscript “st” refers to the standard and “x” to the unknown sample. For these aqueous solutions,
η x
/
η
s t
=
1
, So the equation is simplified to:
Q
x
/
Q
s t
=
S x
S
s t
A
s t
A x
.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Calculation of the minimum detection limit
ce:section.ce:para:
0, 0.00015, 0.00035, 0.0007, 0.0015, 0.0035, 0.007, 0.015, 0.035, 0.07, 0.15, 0.35, 1.5, 3.5, 7 mmol/L of formaldehyde were configured, 1 mL of different concentrations of FA solution, 1 mL of aqueous solution, and 1 mL of 20 mg/mL TA-CDs solution were taken in a four-way cuvette, and fluorescence intensity was detected by fluorescence spectrometer, and scatter plot was drawn for fitting. The limit of detection was calculated using the formula (Wang et al., 2020).
(2)
L
=
3 s / k
where L is the detection limit LOD, S is the standard deviation of the mixed solution of TA-CDs and FA, and k is the slope of the curve fitted to the fluorescence intensity versus formaldehyde concentration.

To analyze the effects of different reaction times, temperatures, and ratios on the fluorescence intensity of carbon dots (CDs), we considered various parameters: reaction times (2 h, 4 h, 6 h, 8 h), reaction temperatures (140 °C, 160 °C, 180 °C, 200 °C), and reaction ratios (1: 0, 1: 2, 1: 4, 1: 6, 1: 8).

Fig. 1 a and 1b illustrate that, with an excitation wavelength of 365 nm, fluorescence intensity increased with both reaction time and temperature. This trend was likely due to the alkaline nature of urea and the acidic nature of the TA solution. As the reaction progresses, longer times and higher temperatures facilitated a more complete reaction between TA and urea, thereby enhancing fluorescence. The reaction reached completion at 6 h and 180 °C; beyond these conditions, no further increased in fluorescence intensity is observed.

Furthermore, when the mass of TA is held constant, the fluorescence intensity increased with the amount of urea, as shown in Fig. 1c. This phenomenon can be attributed to the fact that one molecule of TA can react with multiple molecules of urea. Consequently, a higher concentration of urea led to a more complete reaction, resulting in stronger fluorescence. The reaction achieved completion at a TA to urea ratio of 1: 6. Additionally, the optimal emission wavelength varied significantly when only TA was involved compared to when urea was present, indicating that TA indeed reacts with urea.

TEM images of carbon dots (CDs), as shown in Fig. 2 , revealed that the particles are uniformly distributed with no significant aggregation, and their sizes ranged from 2 to 6 nm. The CDs exhibited a well-resolved lattice spacing of 0.37 nm, corresponding to the (002) crystallographic plane of graphitic carbon, which indicated a high degree of crystallinity and similar core structures (Yi et al., 2023). Fig. 3 displays the TEM images of the carbon dots prepared with varying ratios of TA to urea (1:0, 1:2, 1:4, 1:8). It can be observed that when the ratio is from 1:0 to 1:4, the insufficient reaction due to the low urea content results in smaller carbon dot particle sizes. On the other hand, when the ratio of TA to urea was 1:8, the excessive urea content led to aggregation of the carbon dots and an increase in particle size. It can be inferred that when the ratio is 1:6, the carbon dots are more evenly distributed without aggregation. This is the optimal reaction ratio.

For structural analysis, a comparative study was conducted with TA. Fig. 4 illustrates that TA displays stretching vibrations of –OH at 3373 cm−1 (Pantojacastro and Gonzálezrodríguez, 2011; Erdem et al., 2013), CO stretching at 1716 cm−1 (Wu et al., 2011), phenyl ring skeletal vibrations at 1612, 1533, and 1450 cm−1 (Wu et al., 2011), C-C stretching at 1323 cm−1 (Ruxue et al., 2023); C–O stretching at 1178 cm−1 (Ruxue et al., 2023), and aromatic (C-H) out-of-plane bending at 753 cm−1 (Wang et al., 2024).

The infrared spectrum of carbon dots (CDs) shows stretching vibrations of N-H and O-H at 3206 cm−1 (Yuxin et al., 2021). Conjugation between ester oxygen of ester compounds and amides shifts the CO stretching vibration to 1631 cm−1 (Kong, 2016). The peak at 1593 cm−1 corresponds to CC aromatic stretching (Yusha Ma et al., 2019), while 1561 cm−1 represents N-H bending vibration (Luo et al., 2020), and 1400 cm−1 indicates C-N stretching, confirming successful amino functionalization (Yusha Ma et al., 2019). The sharper peak at 1065 cm−1 for C-O bending suggests a reaction between TA and urea (Yusha Ma et al., 2019). The presence of oxygen- and nitrogen-rich functional groups on the surface likely contributes to preventing AIQ (Zhu et al., 2015). Typically, these functional groups prevent π-π* stacking of sp2 domains of carbon cores in aggregates, thereby avoiding AIQ.

Quinine sulfate (0.1 M H2SO4 as the solvent; QY = 0.54) was chosen as the QY measurements. The quantum yield of CDs in water was calculated using equation (1), resulting in a QY 39.855 % in the aqueous solution.

XPS analysis, as shown in Fig. 5 , indicates that CDs contain elements C, O, and N, with a notably high N content. Since the unreacted TA and urea had been removed during the purification step, it indicated that the product contains the elements C, O, and N. Given that TA itself didn’t contain N, this suggests that a new compound had been formed through the reaction between TA and urea. The high-resolution C1s spectrum reveals characteristic peaks at 284.8 eV (C-C/C-H), 285.94 eV (C-O/C-N), and 288.77 eV (CO) (Yin et al., 2019). The newly formed CO bond indicates the generation of an ester group, which corresponds to the infrared spectrum. In the O1s high-resolution spectrum, peaks corresponding to C-O (532.97 eV) and C = O (531.7 eV) were observed. The number of CO bonds is consistent with the C1s spectrum, being fewer than the C-O bonds. Further analysis of the N1s spectrum shows peaks at 401.61 eV (secondary amine –NH) and 400.03 eV (primary amine –NH2). This indicates that the amino group on urea had been linked to TA molecules, resulting in the partial conversion of some into primary amines and others into secondary amines. Consistent with the structural hypothesis of amino-modified CDs, indicating successful synthesis.

The XRD pattern of carbon dots (Fig. 6 ) shows a broad peak centered around 22°-26°, indicating the presence of lattice defects. This also suggests a certain degree of order in the carbon atom arrangement, consistent with the (002) lattice plane of graphitic carbon (Shi, 2023). The ordered structure may help reduce light scattering and energy loss within the carbon dots, thereby enhancing their luminescence efficiency and stability. Combining the current research focus, a sharp peak indicates a well-ordered crystal structure with high crystallinity. Compared with the graphite carbon of JCPDS card numbers: 98–000-0231, it is found that it corresponds to the crystal plane 002.

The UV absorption analysis of the TA solution (Fig. 7 a) reveals two distinct absorption peaks at 266 nm and 303 nm. These peaks correspond to the π-π* transition of the conjugated CC system in the aromatic ring and the n-π* transition involving C-O surface groups, respectively (Wei et al., 2021). The aromatic structure contributes more significantly to the light absorption. In Fig. 7b, four different concentrations of CDs were tested for their UV absorption spectra. The results indicated that as the concentration increases, the fluorescence intensity of the CDs gradually enhanced. Additionally, a new red-shifted peak appears at 332 nm, which can be attributed to the n-π* transition of the surface state. This suggests that nitrogen doping led to the generation of new surface functional groups (Liu et al., 2017).

The formation of a conjugated aromatic system was likely indicated by this phenomenon. The enhanced spatial distribution of π electrons within the conjugated system as it extends likely caused this. This spatial extension strengthened the interaction between π electron energy levels within the molecule, allowing electrons to move more freely. Consequently, this lowered the energy levels of the electrons, resulting in a redshift of the absorption wavelength (Spano, 2005).

In the fluorescence spectrum (Fig. 8 a), CDs exhibit optimal excitation at 334 nm and emission at 428 nm. The inset shows that the prepared CDs appear lit brown under sunlight and emitted blue fluorescence under UV lamp excitation at 365 nm.

When CDs (Fig. 8b) was excited with different wavelengths, the emission gradually shifted to longer wavelengths with increasing excitation wavelength. This indicates that the prepared CDs exhibit excitation-dependent fluorescence properties. This behavior can be attributed to the presence of multiple luminescent centers within CDs or a mixture of various fluorescent substances, which may include both CDs and molecular fluorophores or their aggregates. Upon excitation of one CDs molecule, energy was transferred via non-radiative processes to another CDs molecule, causing it to fluoresce as well. This energy transfer process depends heavily on the distance and relative orientation between molecules, suggesting that CDs molecules are closely spaced and interact with each other. This phenomenon highlights that the emission from CDs is excitation-dependent (PL), potentially arising from both the intrinsic states of the carbon core and the widespread distribution of PL centers. This property makes CDs suitable for applications in fluorescence detection (Thongsai et al., 2017).

The fluorescence intensity of CDs in aqueous solution varied with pH, as shown in Fig. 8c. It is observed that in the case of strong acid environment, the fluorescence effect is the best. However, the fluorescence intensity decreases in weakly acidic or weakly alkaline conditions (pH 5–9), then gradually increases, and eventually decreases again. This indicates that compared with weak acid and weak base environmenta, neutral environment favors fluorescence enhancement (Ge, 2022). This phenomenon may be attributed to the formation of active fluorescent species or favorable conformational stability of the fluorophores under neutral conditions, leading to stronger fluorescence emission. In contrast, under weakly acidic or alkaline conditions, these active species may not form or may lose stability, resulting in reduced fluorescence. Under strongly acidic conditions, changes in molecular structure or surface properties of the fluorophores may promote more efficient fluorescence emission.

Upon excitation at 365 nm(Fig. 8d), emission spectra were tested for nine different concentrations, revealing that at low concentrations, molecules existed in a monomeric state with low fluorescence efficiency. Conversely, at higher concentrations, molecular aggregation occurred, leading to increased fluorescence. Fluorescence peaked at 20 mg/mL, with further concentration increased resulting in fluorescence quenching. This indicated that the observed CDs exhibited aggregation-induced quenching (AIQ) characteristics, where aggregation at high concentrations altered the fluorescence properties of the molecules. The main source of AIQ in CDs is speculated to arise from radiative recombination due to energy or charge transfer between different luminescent centers or substances within non-aggregated entities (Jian et al., 2024).

By plotting the ratio of the fluorescence intensity (F) of the added formaldehyde at 365 nm to the fluorescence intensity of the blank sample (F0) against the formaldehyde concentration (0–––7 mmol/L), a significant linear relationship is observed within the range of 0.00015–––0.035 mmol/L(Fig. 9 ). The fluorescence intensity gradually decreases as the formaldehyde concentration increases. This phenomenon can be attributed to the interaction between formaldehyde and carbon dots, which altered the structure of the carbon dots and consequently affected their luminescence properties. Weak acids are known to weaken the fluorescence of conjugated molecules, and formaldehyde likely induces a similar effect. Using equation (2), the detection limit of formaldehyde is calculated to be 0.296 μmol/L. Table 1 compares the performance of the synthesized carbon dots with other synthetic carbon dots reported in the literature. The results indicate that the carbon dots synthesized in this study exhibit superior properties for detecting formaldehyde in the air.Table 2 .

This paper reports the synthesis of TA-CDs using TA and urea as raw materials through a hydrothermal method at 180 °C for 6 h, achieving a quantum yield of 39.855 %. Additionally, its fluorescence intensity demonstrates a good linear relationship with the concentration of formaldehyde with a detection limit of 0.296 μmol/L. These results confirm that TA-CDs exhibit high sensitivity in formaldehyde detection, and this capability is of great significance for monitoring indoor air quality and protecting residents' health.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ethics approval and consent to participate
ce:section.ce:para: Not applicable.
The publication of this article has obtained the written informed consent of Beihua University and all authors.
Yaning Li, Jianxi Song wrote the main manuscript text, Guiquan Jiang and Junhua Wu Performed checks, Chuanxi Chi and Yuezhao wrote Figs. 1-9. All the authors reviewed the manuscript.

Yaning Li: Writing – original draft, Methodology. Chuanxi Chi: Data curation. Yue Zhao: Data curation. Guiquan Jiang: Resources. Junhua Wu: Supervision. Jianxi Song: Writing – review & editing, Supervision.

This work was financially supported by Jilin Province fund project (YDZJ202201ZYTS672), Jilin Provincial Development and Reform Commission Project (2021C036-9), Jilin Province Traditional Chinese Medicine Science and Technology Project (2024157), Project of Jilin Provincial Department of Education (JJKH20220079KJ).

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: First of all, I would like to express my gratitude to all those who helped me during the writing of this thesis. I gratefully acknowledge the help of my supervisor, Mr. Song, who has offered me valuable suggestions in the academic studies. In the preparation of this thesis, he has spent much time reading through each draft and provided me with inspiring advice. Without his patient instruction, insightful criticism and expert guidance, the completion of this thesis would not have been possible.
